volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section 5.7.1387 - Section 5.7.1422b)

sections.label: Section 5.7.1387
sections.original: Accedam . per ego tua te genua óbsecro
sections.translation: I ’ ll approach him . ( He falls on the ground before AGORASTOCLES .) By your knees I do beseech you , and by him ( pointing to HANNO .) whom I understand to be your relative ; since you are deserving persons , as it befits deserving persons to do ,

sections.label: Section 5.7.1390
sections.translation: do then come to the aid of your suppliant ! Indeed , already did I know them to be free women , and was waiting for some one to claim their freedom , for really they are none of mine . Then besides , I ’ ll restore your gold that I ’ ve got in my house , and I ’ ll make oath

sections.label: Section 5.7.1395
sections.translation: that I have done nothing , Agorastocles , with ill intent .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1396
sections.original: Quid mihi par facere sit , ego mecum consulam .
sections.translation: As it ’ s right for me to do , I shall still consult my own notions . Let go of my knees .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1397b
sections.translation: I ’ ll let them go , if such is your determination .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1398
sections.translation: Hark you ! Procurer .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1398b
sections.translation: What do you want with a Procurer amid business ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1399
sections.original: Vt minam mi argenti reddas , prius quam in nervom abducere .
sections.translation: You to restore me my money before I take you hence to be laid in fetters .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1400
sections.translation: May the Gods grant better things !

sections.label: Section 5.7.1400b
sections.translation: Even so ; you ’ ll be dining away from home . I see Gold , silver , and your neck , Procurer , the three things are you now owing to me all at once .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1402
sections.original: Quid me in hac re facere deceat , egomet mecum cogito .
sections.translation: What it befits me to do in this matter , I ’ m considering with myself . If I should attempt to take vengeance on this fellow , I shall be engaging in litigation in a strange city . So far as I hear , his disposition and manners , of the nature that they are —

sections.label: Section 5.7.1405
sections.original: Mi pater , ne quid tibi cum istoc rei sit pessumo , obsecro .
sections.translation: My father , do have no dealings with this man , I conjure you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1406
sections.original: Ausculta sorori . abi , díiunge inimicitias cum improbo .
sections.translation: Do listen to my sister . Come , put an end to your strife with the rascal .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1407
sections.original: Hoc age sis , leno . quamquam ego te meruisse ut pereas scio ,
sections.translation: Attend to this , will you , Procurer . Although I know that you deserve to come to ruin , I ’ ll not try the matter with you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1408b
sections.original: Neque ego ; si aurum mihi reddes meum ,
sections.translation: Nor I , if you restore me my gold ; Procurer , when let go from the fetters — you may get thrust into prison .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1410
sections.translation: What , your old habit still ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1410b
sections.original: Ego , Poene , tibi me purgatum volo .
sections.translation: Carthaginian , I wish to excuse myself to you . If I have said anything in my passion against the inclination of your feelings , I beg that you will pardon it ; and as you have found these daughters of yours , so may the Deities bless me , it is a pleasure to me .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1413b
sections.translation: I both forgive and believe you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1414
sections.original: Leno , tu autem amicam mihi des facito aut auri mihi reddas minam .
sections.translation: Procurer , do you take care either to find me a mistress , or return me the mina of gold .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1415
sections.translation: Should you like to have my music-girl ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1415b
sections.translation: I don ’ t care for a music-girl ; you don ’ t know which is the greater , their cheeks or their bosoms .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1417
sections.translation: I ’ ll find one to please you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1417b
sections.translation: Mind that .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1417c
sections.original: Aúrum cras ad te referam tuom .
sections.translation: To-morrow I ’ ll bring back your gold to your house .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1418
sections.translation: Take care that you keep that in memory .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1418b
sections.translation: Captain , follow me .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1418c
sections.translation: Yes , I ’ ll follow you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1419
sections.original: Quid ais , patrue ? quando hinc ire cogitas Carthaginem ?
sections.translation: How say you , kinsman ? When are you thinking of leaving here for Carthage ?—

sections.label: Section 5.7.1420
sections.original: nam tecum mi una ire certum est .
sections.translation: for I ’ m determined to go together with you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1420b
sections.translation: As soon as ever I can , that instant I shall go .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1421
sections.original: Dum auctionem facio , hic opus est aliquot ut maneas dies .
sections.translation: It ’ s necessary for you to stop here some days , until I ’ ve had an auction .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1422
sections.translation: I ’ ll do just as you wish .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1422b
sections.original: Age sis , eamus , nos curemus . plaudite .
sections.translation: Come , please , let ’ s be off ; let ’ s enjoy ourselves . ( To the AUDIENCE .) Grant us your applause .]
